context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. - Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on globalization + government planning capacity. i’m kinda stuck on how to connect governance, city planner vulnerability, and “good governance” without making it a mess.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Before thinking about structure, let's make sure the core idea is solid. In your own words: what do you think 'planning capacity' actually means for a government? What does a government need in order to plan effectively?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and has given a general overview of their confusion. Rather than scaffolding the whole structure for them, I should ask a focused opening question that gets them thinking about the core concept first — the relationship between globalization and planning capacity. This respects the Socratic method and guided discovery principles while keeping the response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: umm i think planning capacity is like a gov’s ability to actually make plans happen, not just write them. so maybe it needs money, legit authority, decent institutions, data, and enough control that outside actors don’t totally derail it?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong foundation — especially the point about outside actors. Now, how do you think globalization specifically affects those ingredients you listed? Does it strengthen or weaken them, and for whom?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid, thoughtful answer that covers most key dimensions of planning capacity. I should affirm what's strong without being sycophantic, and then push them to connect this to globalization specifically — the first part of the prompt. This moves the Socratic questioning in the direction of the solution.

exchanges.student: i think both tbh. globalization can bring investment, ideas, tech, and tax revenue, but it can also make governments more dependent on global markets, donors, firms, and credit ratings, so their room to plan gets squeezed.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that tension between opportunity and dependency is key. Now zoom in: why might city planners specifically be more exposed to those global fluctuations than, say, national-level planners?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking well and has identified the dual nature of globalization's impact on planning capacity. This connects directly to the prompt. Now I should push them toward the more specific question about city planners' vulnerability, which is the next part of the prompt and will help them build toward the governance discussion.

exchanges.student: maybe cuz cities are where global capital lands first? like ports, finance, real estate, outsourcing, tourism etc, so when the global economy shifts, cities feel it fast. i’m not fully sure how to say that better tho.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Now think about this: are all cities equally exposed to those global fluctuations, or does it depend on something? What might make one city more or less vulnerable than another?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition about cities as nodes of global capital flows. I shouldn't help them with language or phrasing per the rules, but I can affirm the core idea and push them to think deeper about the asymmetry — specifically whether all cities are equally vulnerable, which will help them build a more nuanced argument for their paper.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe it depends on how tied the city is to one industry or outside investment? like if a city relies a lot on tourism or export manufacturing, it prob gets hit harder than a more diversified city.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — economic diversification matters a lot there. You've built a solid base on planning capacity and city vulnerability. Now let's tackle the harder part: the prompt asks about 'good governance' and who drives the governance agenda. What do you think 'good governance' actually means in a development context, and where does that definition come from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a solid argument about city vulnerability. They've now covered planning capacity and city vulnerability reasonably well. To avoid dwelling too long on this part, I should acknowledge their progress and pivot toward the governance part of the prompt, which is the most complex and underdeveloped section so far.

exchanges.student: this part is where i get fuzzy ngl. i think “good governance” means transparency, accountability, rule of law, participation, efficiency stuff like that, but i’m guessing a lot of that definition comes from donors and intl institutions, not just local actors.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation. So if the definition is largely coming from external actors like the World Bank or IMF — what are the stakes of that for local planners? What gets complicated when 'good governance' is introduced from the outside?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both the content of 'good governance' and the key tension about who defines it. This is a crucial insight that connects to the course themes about external actors and power/legitimacy. I should push them to think about the implications of that observation — specifically what it means when the definition is externally imposed, which will help them address the 'stakes' question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: i think the risk is it sounds universal but can ignore local politics/history. like planners might get pushed to follow donor-friendly reforms for legitimacy/funding even if those reforms weaken local autonomy or don’t fit actual needs.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important tension — the legitimacy trap, essentially. Now here's the hardest question in the prompt: is governance failure a cause or a consequence of globalization? What do you think, and can you argue both sides?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent point about conditionality and the tension between external legitimacy and local autonomy — this connects directly to the course themes about power, legitimacy, and external actors. Now I should push them toward the final and most conceptually challenging question in the prompt: is governance failure a cause or consequence of globalization? This will help them build toward a conclusion for their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: i think it’s both, which is annoying lol. weak governance can make countries more vulnerable to bad globalization effects, but globalization can also overload or bypass institutions and create governance problems.
exchanges.tutor: Good — and that 'both' answer is actually more defensible than picking one side, as long as you can support it with concrete reasoning. You've now worked through all the main parts of the prompt. At this point, I'd encourage you to try pulling it all together into a first draft. What would your opening argument be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the bidirectional relationship, which is the sophisticated answer the prompt is looking for. They've now worked through all the major components of the prompt. Given we're at exchange 8 of 10, and they've built solid ideas on all the key questions, I should push them to start integrating everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss individual pieces. This follows the redundancy rule and the instruction to ask students to integrate their thinking into written content.

exchanges.student: maybe my opening claim is: globalization doesn’t just reduce planning capacity; it reshapes it unevenly, giving governments and cities new resources while also making them more constrained by global markets and external governance norms. does that sound too broad?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening — it captures the tension well. Ask yourself: does it signal to the reader that you'll also be addressing the governance agenda and who drives it? Does your claim need to hint at that thread too?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has crafted a strong opening claim that captures the dual nature of globalization's impact and hints at the unevenness/inequality dimension. I should not evaluate it as a grade but give honest feedback. The claim is good but I can push them to think about whether it fully sets up ALL the threads they need to address — particularly the governance agenda question — without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah prob. maybe i should add that planning capacity gets reshaped not only by markets but also by donor/institutional ideas of “good governance”? i’m trying not to make the thesis too bloated tho.
exchanges.tutor: That balance is worth wrestling with in your draft — you don't need to solve it here. You've built solid ideas on every part of the prompt. Time to start writing and let the structure emerge. What's stopping you from putting a first draft together now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking carefully about thesis construction and has the right instinct about not over-bloating it. They've done strong conceptual work across all parts of the prompt. Since we're at exchange 10, I should encourage them to commit to a direction and start writing, rather than continuing to refine the thesis in conversation. I should not write or suggest language for them.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic dialogue throughout all ten turns. Every key idea — planning capacity, city vulnerability, good governance, the governance agenda, and the cause/consequence question — was drawn out from the student through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on the student's prior answers, kept responses concise and focused, maintained a warm and encouraging tone, and never crossed into providing submission-ready content. No deductions are warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready content; all substantive claims (e.g., the legitimacy trap, diversification as a vulnerability factor, the bidirectional governance-globalization relationship) were generated by the student. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each student response before advancing to the next concept, and used diagnostic questions rather than information dumps. Under 1.3, the tutor provided process-level guidance in Turn 9 and Turn 10 by prompting the student to check whether the thesis signals all required threads, which is method-level feedback. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment drift, and no off-topic engagement. Communication was bite-sized and interactive throughout, with a consistently supportive, non-evaluative tone.
